runs.reasoningSummary: The literature consistently suggests that the intensive-margin income elasticity (the pure income effect) for prime-age workers is negative but small in magnitude. While estimates vary slightly by demographic, the consensus in standard labor economics literature is that while non-labor income reduces labor supply due to the preference for leisure, the response is generally quite inelastic.

runs.interpretation: The percentage change in annual hours worked by currently employed prime-age workers in the US in response to a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage constant.
citations: Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) Handbook of Labor Economics
Chetty et al. (2011) QJE
Pencavel (1986) Handbook of Labor Economics
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the intensive-margin income effect for prime-age workers are consistently small and negative, often statistically indistinguishable from zero or very close to it. While theory suggests leisure is a normal good (implying a negative elasticity), empirical identification is difficult due to the confounding nature of wealth shocks. Most modern labor supply literature points to a very weak negative response, significantly smaller in absolute magnitude than the compensated wage elasticity.

  "interpretation": "The percentage change in annual hours worked by currently employed prime-age workers in the US in response to a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage constant.",

    "p25": -0.08,

  },

    "Chetty et al. (2011) QJE",
    "Pencavel (1986) Handbook of Labor Economics"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical estimates of the intensive-margin income effect for prime-age workers are consistently small and negative, often statistically indistinguishable from zero or very close to it. While theory suggests leisure is a normal good (implying a negative elasticity), empirical identification is difficult due to the confounding nature of wealth shocks. Most modern labor supply literature points to a very weak negative response, significantly smaller in absolute magnitude than the compensated wage elasticity."
}
